code: @Manual{,   title = {erlr: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models},   author = {Danielle Navarro},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.9.9000},   url = {https://github.com/djnavarro/erlr}, }

what: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
text: Provides estimation plotting tools exposure-response models use logistic regression binary responses. mostly intended convenience package: core tools wrappers around glm(), plotting tools use ggplot2 patchwork build typical plots used exposure-response modelling.

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
text: can install development version erlr like :
code: pak::pak("djnavarro/erlr")

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
code: library(erlr) library(tibble)  lr_data #> # A tibble: 300 × 7 #>       id  dose exposure_1 quartile_1 response_1 response_2 sex    #>    <int> <dbl>      <dbl> <fct>           <dbl>      <dbl> <fct>  #>  1     1   100      148.  Q3                  1          1 Male   #>  2     2   100       79.7 Q1                  1          0 Male   #>  3     3   200      212.  Q3                  1          0 Male   #>  4     4   200      236.  Q3                  0          0 Female #>  5     5     0        0   Placebo             1          0 Male   #>  6     6   200       71.0 Q1                  1          0 Female #>  7     7   100      173.  Q3                  1          0 Male   #>  8     8   100      123.  Q2                  0          0 Female #>  9     9     0        0   Placebo             0          0 Male   #> 10    10   200      165.  Q3                  1          0 Female #> # ℹ 290 more rows  mod <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1, lr_data) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = stats::binomial(link = "logit"),  #>     data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)   exposure_1   #>     0.15078      0.01112   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:       341.7  #> Residual Deviance: 283.9     AIC: 287.9

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
code: lr_data |>    lr_plot(exposure_1, response_1) |>    lr_plot_show_model() |>    lr_plot_show_quantiles() |>    lr_plot_show_groups(dose) |>    plot() lr_data |>     lr_plot(exposure_1, response_1, color_by = sex) |>     lr_plot_show_model(use_color = FALSE) |>     lr_plot_show_quantiles(bins = 3) |>     lr_plot_show_datastrip() |>     lr_plot_show_groups(group_by = c(quartile_1, dose), use_color = FALSE) |>     plot()

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
code: mod1 <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1 + sex + dose, lr_data) mod2 <- lr_scm_backward(mod1, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) lr_scm_history(mod2) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model <NA>   <NA>        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 2         1       1 backward   remove ~dose       response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 3         1       2 backward   remove ~sex        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 4         2       3 backward   remove ~sex        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
code: mod <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1 + sex, lr_data) sim <- lr_vpc_sim(mod, seed = 1234) sim #> # A tibble: 30,000 × 5 #>    response_1 exposure_1 sex    row_id sim_id #>         <dbl>      <dbl> <fct>   <int>  <int> #>  1      0.918      148.  Male        1      1 #>  2      0.823       79.7 Male        2      1 #>  3      0.962      212.  Male        3      1 #>  4      0.929      236.  Female      4      1 #>  5      0.625        0   Male        5      1 #>  6      0.611       71.0 Female      6      1 #>  7      0.940      173.  Male        7      1 #>  8      0.755      123.  Female      8      1 #>  9      0.625        0   Male        9      1 #> 10      0.841      165.  Female     10      1 #> # ℹ 29,990 more rows  lr_vpc_plot(mod, sim, group_by = exposure_1) lr_vpc_plot(mod, sim, group_by = sex)

text: formula Model formula data Data set ... arguments passed glm()

title: Fit a logistic regression function — lr_model
code: mod <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1, lr_data) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = stats::binomial(link = "logit"),  #>     data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)   exposure_1   #>     0.15078      0.01112   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:	    341.7  #> Residual Deviance: 283.9 	AIC: 287.9

what: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
title: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
text: Builds exposure-response plot logistic regression model

title: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
code: lr_plot(data, exposure, response, color_by = NULL)  lr_plot_style(object, labels)  lr_plot_show_model(object, use_color = NULL, conf_level = 0.95)  lr_plot_show_quantiles(object, use_color = NULL, bins = 4, conf_level = 0.95)  lr_plot_show_datastrip(   object,   use_color = NULL,   style = "jitter",   panel = "both" )  lr_plot_show_groups(object, group_by, use_color = NULL)  lr_plot_build(object)

title: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
text: data Observed data exposure Exposure variable (one variable, unquoted) response Response variable (one variable, unquoted) color_by Stratification variable used color fill (one variable, unquoted) object Partially constructed plot (S3 class erlr_plot) labels Named list labels use_color Logical, indicating whether component keep color stratification conf_level Confidence level bins Number exposure bins (counting placebo) style Character string: "jitter" (default) "dotplot" panel Character string: "upper", "lower", "" (default) group_by Stratification variables define groups boxplots (tidyselection variables)

title: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
text: Plot object class erlr_plot

title: Builds an exposure-response plot for a logistic regression model — lr_plot
code: lr_data |>    lr_plot(exposure_1, response_1) |>    lr_plot_show_model() |>    lr_plot_show_quantiles() |>    lr_plot_show_groups(quartile_1) |>    plot()   lr_data |>    lr_plot(exposure_1, response_1, sex) |>    lr_plot_show_model() |>    lr_plot_show_quantiles() |>    lr_plot_show_datastrip() |>    lr_plot_show_groups(quartile_1) |>    plot()   #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Sex"   lr_data |>    lr_plot(exposure_1, response_1, color_by = sex) |>    lr_plot_show_model(use_color = FALSE) |>    lr_plot_show_quantiles(bins = 3) |>    lr_plot_show_datastrip() |>    lr_plot_show_groups(group_by = c(quartile_1, dose), use_color = FALSE) |>    plot() #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Sex" #> • fill : "Sex" #> Ignoring unknown labels: #> • colour : "Sex" #> • fill : "Sex"

title: Predictions and confidence intervals for logistic regression — lr_predict
code: mod <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1, lr_data) prd <- lr_predict(mod, lr_data) prd #> # A tibble: 300 × 12 #>       id  dose exposure_1 quartile_1 response_1 response_2 sex    fit_link #>    <int> <dbl>      <dbl> <fct>           <dbl>      <dbl> <fct>     <dbl> #>  1     1   100      148.  Q3                  1          1 Male      1.80  #>  2     2   100       79.7 Q1                  1          0 Male      1.04  #>  3     3   200      212.  Q3                  1          0 Male      2.50  #>  4     4   200      236.  Q3                  0          0 Female    2.77  #>  5     5     0        0   Placebo             1          0 Male      0.151 #>  6     6   200       71.0 Q1                  1          0 Female    0.940 #>  7     7   100      173.  Q3                  1          0 Male      2.08  #>  8     8   100      123.  Q2                  0          0 Female    1.52  #>  9     9     0        0   Placebo             0          0 Male      0.151 #> 10    10   200      165.  Q3                  1          0 Female    1.99  #> # ℹ 290 more rows #> # ℹ 4 more variables: se_link <dbl>, fit_resp <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, #> #   ci_upper <dbl>

what: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
title: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
text: Stepwise covariate modelling logistic regression

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
code: lr_scm_forward(mod, candidates, threshold = 0.01, seed = NULL)  lr_scm_backward(mod, candidates, threshold = 0.001, seed = NULL)  lr_scm_history(mod)

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
text: mod erlr model object candidates Character vector list candidate terms threshold Threshold test seed Optional seed control order term tests

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
text: lr_scm_forward() lr_scm_backward(), updated erlr model returned, SCM history log updated internally. lr_scm_history(), data frame returned containing SCM history log

title: Stepwise covariate modelling for logistic regression — lr_scm
code: mod0 <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1, lr_data) mod1 <- lr_scm_forward(mod0, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) lr_scm_history(mod1) #> # A tibble: 3 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model NA     NA          response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 2         1       1 forward    add    ~sex        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 3         1       2 forward    add    ~dose       response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>  mod2 <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1 + sex + dose, lr_data) mod3 <- lr_scm_backward(mod2, candidates = c("sex", "dose")) lr_scm_history(mod3) #> # A tibble: 4 × 11 #>   iteration attempt step       action term_tested model_tested   model_converged #>       <int>   <int> <chr>      <chr>  <chr>       <chr>          <lgl>           #> 1         0       0 base model NA     NA          response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 2         1       1 backward   remove ~dose       response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 3         1       2 backward   remove ~sex        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> 4         2       3 backward   remove ~sex        response_1 ~ … TRUE            #> # ℹ 4 more variables: term_p_value <dbl>, model_aic <dbl>, model_bic <dbl>, #> #   model_updated <int>

what: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
title: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
text: Simulate logistic regression model

title: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
code: lr_simulator(object)

title: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
text: object logistic regression model

title: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
text: function arguments param, data, type. param argument vector coefficients data argument data frame tibble type argument string indicating type prediction generate (defaults "response") Takes fitted glm object input returns function evaluate underlying structural model user-specified parameters data (e.g., VPCs counterfactual simulation scenarios). principle work glms generally, merely logistic regressions, tested except logistic regression models

title: Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
code: mod1 <- lr_model(response_1 ~ exposure_1 + sex, lr_data) par1 <- coef(mod1) mod1_sim <- lr_simulator(mod1)  # no counterfactuals p1 <- mod1_sim(param = par1, data = lr_data)  p2 <- unname(predict(mod1, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the data set lr_data2 <- lr_data[1:20, ] p3 <- mod1_sim(param = par1, data = lr_data2)  p4 <- unname(predict(mod1, newdata = lr_data2, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the parameters par2 <- par1 int1 <- par1["(Intercept)"] par2["(Intercept)"] <- 0 p5 <- mod1_sim(param = par2, data = lr_data)
